title: Considering Job
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Why Study Job Now?
subpoints: The church is not currently in acute suffering — the best time to prepare for what will come
The phone call will come, the diagnosis will be given — suffering interrupts all human lives
Better to understand suffering before being in the midst of it than during
Job equips us to suffer well ourselves and to minister more effectively to others who suffer

outline.title: Seven Questions About the Book of Job
subpoints: Who wrote it? God through an unknown, skilled human author well-versed in wisdom literature
When? Approximately 2000 BC, the age of the patriarchs — God’s names, Job’s wealth, and his age all point there
Where? The land of Uz, likely modern northern Saudi Arabia or southern Jordan — among the nations, not Israel
How is it structured? Historical prologue (1-2), lament (3), three cycles of dialogue (4-27), summary (28-31), Elihu (32-37), God speaks (38-41), epilogue (42)

outline.title: The Literary Genre of Job
subpoints: Most diverse literary mix in the Bible: prose, poetry, dialogue, wisdom, prophecy, apocalyptic
Job is historical — Ezekiel and James treat him as a real person alongside Noah and Daniel
It is not a parable or allegory; treating it as such undermines biblical inerrancy
Each section must be interpreted according to its own genre, especially the friends’ speeches

outline.title: Why Was the Book Written?
subpoints: Not primarily to provide a theodicy, though it touches on God’s goodness amid evil
God never explains himself to Job — he corrects Job’s misunderstanding and rebukes his arrogance
Primary purpose: to correct the dangerous assumption that good things happen to good people and bad to bad
The most righteous person who ever lived suffered the greatest suffering — the Lord Jesus Christ

outline.title: Why Should We Care About This Book?
subpoints: It is in the Bible — one of the six wisdom books given for our instruction
Job deals with subject matter that will touch every life; every Christian will suffer
It provides practical examples of what to do and not to do when ministering to the suffering
It reveals the glory of Jesus in subtle but powerful ways as the story of the suffering servant

summary:
This Sunday School lesson opens a new series on the book of Job with a sweeping introductory session. The teacher begins by explaining why the church is studying Job now: precisely because the congregation is not currently in acute suffering, it is the best moment to prepare for what will surely come. The phone call will arrive, the diagnosis will be given, life will change in an instant — and the book of Job exists to help believers suffer well when that moment arrives, and to equip them to minister to others who are in the middle of it.

The bulk of the session works through seven introductory questions about the book: authorship (unknown human author, ultimately God; skilled in wisdom literature), date (approximately 2000 BC, the patriarchal age, based on God’s names, Job’s wealth, his lifespan, and the absence of a Levitical priesthood), location (the land of Uz, likely modern Saudi Arabia or southern Jordan, significantly outside the promised land, suggesting an intentionally universal message), structure (a detailed outline of the book’s seven main sections), literary genre (the most diverse in the Bible: prose, poetry, dialogue, wisdom, apocalyptic), and purpose.

The purpose of the book is given special attention. Job is not primarily a theodicy, the teacher argues, because God does not explain himself to Job at the end. Rather, God corrects a wrong but common human assumption: that good things happen to good people and bad things to bad people. Biblical theology is not a mathematical formula. The righteous can and do suffer — and the supreme demonstration of this is the Lord Jesus Christ, the most righteous person to have ever lived, who suffered the greatest suffering in history. The series is framed as preparation for the inevitable encounter with suffering, with the hope of becoming people who can both suffer well and minister well to others in their suffering.
transcript:
## Introduction: Why Study Job?

Tonight we’re going to start a series on the book of Job and it’s going to be a little different than the usual series that we do through the book, in that we’re not going to consider every single verse of the book. For example, once we get to chapter four through 27 and so on, those 24 chapters, we’re going to have two lessons on them. And that happens to be the most difficult part of the book as well, so we’re just going to breathe through it and not have to figure out exactly what’s going on there.

But why is it that I thought it would be important for us to look at the book of Job right now in the life of our church? Maybe it’s because currently we’re not going through a lot of suffering, at least not a lot of acute suffering in our church. You know, everybody’s dealing with something in their lives but there’s not a lot of acute suffering going on. And when that happens we pretend that our lives will proceed really uninterrupted by trials or sorrow, but the reality is that the phone call will come. The diagnosis will be given, loneliness will strike, life will change in an instant. Suffering will interject in our lives. And it’s better to talk about suffering before you’re in the midst of it than during.

And specifically, as we talk about suffering and the sovereignty of God, I’m not sure that it is super encouraging to stand at the bedside in the hospital of somebody dying and just say that whatever is happening is God’s sovereignty. That’s true, and there’s a measure of comfort there, but perhaps there are other things that we could be doing at that time as well. And as we look at the book of Job, these are the type of questions that we want to be asking and answering.

How do we prepare for these inevitable moments in our lives? How do we prepare to help others as they experience and endure such moments? Are we going to be like the three friends of Job or is there a better approach to helping people? What do we say? How can we help? Will we be ready when suffering comes to us? How will we deal with it and make sense of it? How will we persevere through it? Especially as acute suffering becomes chronic suffering. Somebody dies and the first few weeks everybody is around. Everybody’s bringing meals. Everybody’s concerned. But then people have to go their own ways. And how do we deal with suffering at that point where the void becomes even more present?

One thing we need to realize is that the reality of suffering is common to the human experience. If you’re a human, you’re going to experience suffering at some point in your life. The existence of human suffering is one of the most powerful obstacles to accepting the reality that there is a good and all-powerful God. And yet God in his goodness gives us an entire book of the Bible, the book of Job, dealing with this issue. And it’s a big book too — 41 chapters.

I have three angles as we go through this series. One is I want us to know the book of Job better and be able to benefit from it in our lives. Of all the books of the Bible, Job is the one that I have not known what to do with, particularly those middle chapters from chapter 4 through 27. Job is a very difficult book to apply because almost everyone, including Job, is wrong at the end. So it’s difficult to figure out: are they right here or are they wrong? That’s why you often only hear sermons about Job 1 and 2 and then chapter 38 to the end. Because when God is speaking you’re pretty sure he’s right, so you don’t have to worry about that.

Secondly, I want us to be able to glorify God as both sovereign and good despite the reality of human suffering. I want us to see that human suffering does not deny the goodness and the sovereignty of God. And thirdly, I want to prepare us to better understand and deal with the suffering that we may experience in our lives, while at the same time equipping us to more effectively minister to others who may be experiencing suffering. As Paul says in 2 Corinthians 1, trials and suffering come to your life so that you can be comforted by the God of all comfort, and then turn around and comfort others with the comfort that you received from God during that time.

## Job Is a Challenging Book

Job is a challenging book to study and difficult to do what it says — difficult to accept what it says concerning suffering and God. And it is challenging because it’s a big book: 41 chapters, some of them fairly long. There are only a few books in the Bible that are longer than Job. But it’s also challenging because it conveys its message through a mixture of prose, poetry, and wisdom, and employs a lot of repetition.

In an age of tweets and emoji, we struggle to stick with a book whose message can only be accessed by taking time to consider its poetry, reflect on its repetition, and meditate on its wisdom. So that’s a challenge in understanding Job.

In addition to that, if you are currently experiencing suffering in your life, you will find part of what the book of Job has to say very challenging. There will be times when we will simply not want to hear what the book of Job has to say. So as we go through this glorious and challenging book, I will not promise that we will always find an answer, and I’m not going to promise that the answers that we do find will always be satisfying to you. But I think I can promise you that Job will reveal profound truths to us if we listen attentively to its message.

## Seven Questions About the Book of Job

Now before you go on a trip to an unfamiliar place it’s good to get the lay of the land, maybe ask for directions. If you know where you’re going, look at some maps. The book of Job may be a new land for a lot of us. So let’s take some time to look at some basic information about the book of Job that will help us to understand its message better. We’re going to do that by asking seven questions.

### Question 1: Who Wrote the Book of Job?

Well, at the most basic level — God did. God is the ultimate writer of the Scriptures. So in some sense, it’s irrelevant who the human author was, because you know that it came from God. But in another sense, if we know who wrote it, we can actually better understand the literary sources and so on. We do know that God chooses to compose his message through the agency of inspired human authors. As it says in 2 Peter 1:21: for prophecy never came by the will of man, but holy men of God spoke as they were moved by the Holy Spirit.

So the ultimate author is God, but God used people to give us his word. And those men were not robots — their personality comes through. The best way to see that is in the four Gospels. They’re telling the same story and yet you can see each one’s personality coming through.

Sometimes we don’t know who the human author is. And that’s the case with Job — it is technically an anonymous book as far as a human author goes. The Talmud says Moses wrote Job. Why? I don’t know — somebody one day just decided that Moses wrote it and then they wrote it down and that was it. Other scholars suggest Solomon because there’s so much wisdom literature. Others say Job wrote Job. Others say Elihu wrote Job. The reality is that nobody knows who the human author of Job was.

Now although we don’t know the name of the author, we know a couple of things about him. We know he was a good writer. The book of Job is good writing in the original language. Given the complex nature of the literary forms in the book, we can say that the author is clearly accomplished and skilled in wisdom literature. We also know that the author was chosen to be witness to an extraordinary thing: how many of us have been able to peek into heaven and see what God is talking to his angels about? This author was given that. James Durham, a Scottish Presbyterian Puritan in the 17th century, wrote a short commentary on the book of Job — and he says: be the writer who will, it is the Spirit who is the inditer who has left it to us for a spiritual jewel. And that’s exactly what the book of Job is.

### Question 2: When Did the Events Take Place?

The book seems to fit the general age of the patriarchs — the age in the Bible described from Genesis 12 through Genesis 50. So we’re talking about 500-600 years before the Exodus, which was about 1450 BC. So we’re talking about 2000 BC, give or take.

There are four reasons why I think this is the case. First, the divine names of God in the book of Job are the same kinds of names used in Genesis 12 through 50 — there’s a literary parallelism there. Second, the means of expressing the extent of Job’s wealth also correspond to the patriarchal age — his possessions were 7,000 sheep, 3,000 camels, 500 yoke of oxen and 500 female donkeys and a very large household. The way that wealth is counted here is very similar to what you read in Genesis 12 through 50 as well. Third, Job dies at 140 years of age (Job 42:16), which is kind of equivalent to the lifespans of the later patriarchs. And fourth, there’s no explicit or implicit mention of the Levitical priesthood — in Job 1:5, we find Job himself sacrificing for his kids. That tells us there’s no Levitical priesthood yet, putting him in the time of the patriarchs.

### Question 3: Where Did the Events Take Place?

The book says that Job lived in the land of Uz. The only problem is nobody knows exactly where Uz was. We try to put it together by deduction from other passages. The first mention is in Genesis 10, where Uz is the name of a person, not a city, the son of Aram, grandson of Shem. And then Lamentations mentions the land of Uz, equating it with Edom — the nation that came from Esau, the brother of Jacob. When you put that all together, the scholars tend to come to the consensus that Uz was somewhere in either Saudi Arabia or in southern Jordan.

Notice where it is: it’s not in the promised land. It’s not where the other patriarchs were at the time. It’s outside of Palestine, among the nations. And I think that’s not accidental. God doesn’t do anything without a purpose. This placement indicates that this message of suffering is not just for a particular people. It’s a universal message. What God has to say about human suffering is for the whole world.

### Question 4: How Is the Book of Job Structured?

The book of Job is structured in seven parts. Chapters 1 and 2 form the historical narrative prologue in prose. Then chapter 3 shifts to poetry — Job’s lament over what just happened, visceral and poetic. Look at verse 11 of chapter 3: Job says, why did I not die at birth? Why did I not perish when I came from the womb? That’s the character of this opening lament.

Then chapters 4 through 27 are the most difficult part: three cycles of dialogue between Job and his friends. And in many of his responses, Job is actually addressing God rather than the friends. Look at chapter 7 verse 17 for a second, where Job says, what is man that you should exalt him, that you should set your heart on him, that you should visit him every morning and test him every moment? He’s addressing God, not Eliphaz. He’s complaining to God throughout the book.

Then in chapters 28 through 31, if it wasn’t enough, Job summarizes all the arguments as to why the friends and God were wrong. Then a new person comes in — Elihu — in chapters 32 through 37. He rebukes both the three friends and Job. Most people think Elihu was a bad guy, but since he’s never rebuked at the end while the three friends and Job are, I tend to think he was a good guy.

Then God finally intervenes in chapters 38 through 41, and you notice that God mostly asks rhetorical questions. He doesn’t give Job a lot of answers. He doesn’t really tell him exactly why he was suffering. But he does ask him a lot of questions. And then the book ends with the epilogue where God renders his verdict on Job and his friends and then he restores Job.

### Question 5: What Is the Literary Genre?

The thing with Job is that it has all kinds of literary genres. It’s the book of the Bible with the most diverse genres. It has poetry, historical narrative, dialogue, prose, prophecy, and even some apocalyptic passages. So each little part of it has to be interpreted according to its genre.

But it is a historical book. The Bible itself refers to Job as a historical person. Both in Ezekiel and in James, Job is referred to as a historical person alongside Noah and Daniel. I say that because some people are now suggesting that the book of Job is just a parable, that these things never happened. But if you say that, you’re willing to accept that the Bible’s own treatment of Job as a historical figure is wrong. The inerrancy of Scripture is at stake.

### Question 6: Why Was the Book Written?

Historically scholars have said that the book of Job was written to provide a theodicy — an effort to reconcile God’s goodness and justice with the existence of human suffering and evil. Job does some of that, but that’s not really its main purpose. The ultimate purpose for this book is not to provide an apologetic for the goodness of God. Because when God comes in at the end, what does he do? He does very little explaining of himself. He never answers the question that Job has been asking the entire book: why have I suffered?

What God is doing is correcting Job’s misunderstanding of the world, rebuking Job for his arrogance, and demonstrating that Satan’s accusation against God and Job was incorrect. And really, ultimately, what God is trying to do here is to correct a wrong but common human inclination: the inclination to always think that good things happen to good people and bad things happen to bad people. If I do everything right, everything is going to be great for me. We innately believe in a Joel Osteen sort of theology. And the book of Proverbs does tell us that generally, if you walk in the way of the Lord, he’s going to bless, and if you don’t, he’s not going to bless. But good people suffer too. Biblical theology is not a mathematical formula. The righteous can and do suffer. And sometimes they suffer even when they do everything right. The most righteous person to have ever lived suffered the greatest suffering in the history of humanity — and that’s the Lord Jesus Christ.

### Question 7: Why Should We Care About This Book?

One, because it’s in the Bible — one of the six wisdom books that God has given us. Two, because Job deals with the subject matter that will touch all of our lives. Every Christian will suffer. Every person will suffer. Thirdly, this book provides Christians with practical advice and examples of what to do and what not to do when ministering to people who are suffering. And Job reveals to us the glory of Jesus in subtle, indirect but incredibly powerful ways — as we’re going to see — that the book of Job foretells the gospel, the story of the suffering servant. That is one of the reasons why we should be excited about studying and appropriating this book.

Any questions before we close in prayer? Father, thank you for this book. We pray that we would be a people who are able to suffer well and that we will be able to minister to those who are suffering. We pray that you’d bless our journey through this book and that you’d prepare us for your day. Take us home safely, bring us back into your house to worship you with our brothers and sisters. We pray in Jesus’ name. Amen.
